draft.introduction: Tatian’s Diatessaron (c. 170 CE) is the earliest known gospel harmony, weaving the four canonical Gospels into a single continuous narrative. It was the standard gospel text in Syriac-speaking Christianity for over two centuries.

translation.text: In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God. This was in the beginning with God. All things were made through him, and without him was not anything made that was made. In him was life, and the life was the light of men. And the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness did not overcome it.
translation.original_text: [Syriac/Greek reconstruction from Arabic and Ephrem’s commentary]

translator_notes.note: Tatian chose to open his harmony with the Johannine Prologue rather than Matthew’s genealogy or Luke’s preface, a theological choice that privileged cosmic Christology over historical narrative. The original Syriac is lost; the text survives primarily through Ephrem’s commentary and an Arabic translation. Its destruction was ordered by Bishop Theodoret in the fifth century, who considered four separate Gospels theologically necessary.
